title: On the Value of Infinite Gradients in Variational Autoencoder Models

reviews.summary: The paper investigates a specific work regime for VAE characterized by close to infinite gradients. The authors states that this phenomenon occurs for small values of a regularization power $\gamma$ and cannot be avoided, if one wants a perfect data reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:
- Clear analysis of presented phenomenon with a theorem, nice illustrations, profound investigation.

- Present explanation of a well-known effect during the VAE training.

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?
Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: not applicable
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper investigates a specific work regime for VAE characterized by close to infinite gradients. The authors states that this phenomenon occurs for small values of a regularization power $\gamma$ and cannot be avoided, if one wants a perfect data reconstruction.

Main Review:
Pros:
- Clear analysis of presented phenomenon with a theorem, nice illustrations, profound investigation.

- Present explanation of a well-known effect during the VAE training.

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?
Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?

not applicable

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the role of unbounded gradients in variational autoencoders (VAEs), particularly when the decoder variance parameter γ approaches zero. Rather than treating such gradients as pathological artifacts to be avoided, the authors argue that they are actually necessary for achieving optimal sparse latent representations on low-dimensional manifolds. The main contributions are: (1) a formal definition of optimal sparse representations as reconstructions with zero error using the minimal number of informative latent dimensions, (2) a proof (Theorem 4) that any autoencoder-based objective with bounded gradients cannot guarantee optimal sparse representations across all data and decoder configurations, and (3) evidence that learning γ during training provides smoothing benefits that mitigate optimization challenges while localizing problematic unbounded gradients to regions near convergence.

## Strengths

**Originality and Conceptual Contribution**: The paper addresses an underexplored aspect of VAE training and reframes unbounded gradients from a pathology to a necessity. This perspective shift is genuinely novel and challenges conventional wisdom in the field. The work complements existing literature on posterior collapse by addressing the complementary regime of under-regularization, filling an important gap in our theoretical understanding of VAE behavior. The connection between infinite gradients and sparse latent representations appears to be new, and the theoretical framework is well-motivated.

**Clear Theoretical Analysis**: The paper presents a clear analysis of the phenomenon with rigorous mathematical treatment. Theorem 4 is well-stated and the proof strategy via counterexample is appropriate for establishing the necessity of unbounded gradients. The intuition provided in Section 3.3 and the geometric visualizations (Figures 1-2) effectively convey why bounded gradients fail. Lemmas 2-3 provide useful connections between the generic objective and VAE losses, establishing how the results transfer to practical VAE training. The definition of optimal sparse representations (Definition 1) precisely articulates the notion being studied.

**Honest Treatment of Limitations**: The authors are commendably transparent about the restrictiveness of their assumptions. Remarks 2-4 appropriately acknowledge that the lossless reconstruction assumption is restrictive, that the inapplicability to adversarial outliers exists, and that unbounded gradients are necessary but not sufficient conditions. The caveat regarding posterior collapse when γ is learned (end of Section 4) is appropriately noted.

## Major Weaknesses

**Limited Scope and Restrictive Assumptions**: While the theoretical analysis is rigorous within its domain, the scope is significantly constrained. Definition 1 assumes perfect reconstruction (zero error), which is unrealistic for real data with noise or when lossy compression is preferable. The theory further assumes sufficient decoder capacity and data lying on a known low-dimensional manifold—assumptions that are strong and often violated in practice. The gap between these idealized theoretical guarantees and practical finite-capacity networks trained on real-world data remains substantial and underdeveloped. The analysis is restricted to Gaussian VAEs with specific decoder parameterizations; extensions to non-Gaussian posteriors, hierarchical VAEs, or normalizing flow variants are mentioned only briefly.

**Narrow Explanation of the Phenomenon**: The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice, the infinite gradients phenomenon may occur due to different reasons (numerical instabilities, specific architectural choices, learning rate schedules, etc.), so there is no guarantee that identifying this particular mechanism fully solves the problem in real applications. The counterexample underlying Theorem 4 is somewhat artificial (a labyrinthine manifold construction), and its relevance to typical deep learning scenarios could be better justified. The paper does not empirically demonstrate that bounded-gradient alternatives fail in practice; rather, it shows they fail in principle for specific theoretical constructions.

**Insufficient Empirical Validation**: The experimental section is notably limited. The main empirical contribution is Figure 4, comparing learned γ versus fixed γ on CelebA—a single dataset with limited metrics (reconstruction error and MMD). The paper lacks: (a) experiments on diverse datasets and architectures, (b) ablation studies examining sensitivity to network architecture, latent dimension, or initialization, (c) comparisons with alternative approaches to handling unbounded gradients (gradient clipping, second-order optimization methods, adaptive learning rates), and (d) rigorous evaluation of the outlier detection application mentioned in Section 2.2. The toy example in Figure 3 is illustrative but limited in scope. More extensive experiments would substantially strengthen claims about practical benefits and the necessity of unbounded gradients.

**Unresolved Technical Concerns**: Several technical issues require clarification:

1. **Formula (6)**: The presence of h() in the first term is non-standard. Why is this function necessary for the training of autoencoders? Are there alternative criteria for autoencoder learning that don't require the assumptions of Theorem 4 to achieve perfect reconstruction? Under which conditions on data does x^(i) = μ_x(z^(i),θ) require that ||z_k||_2 > 0 for at least r < d rows?

2. **Assumption in Formula (4)**: The interpolation property seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAEs and statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A thorough discussion is needed. Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon if we do not require the interpolation property?

3. **Lemma 2 Restrictions**: The requirement that σ_z(x;φ) = s (constant) is not obvious. What happens if we model σ_z with a neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Might adaptive σ_z compensate for the instability and eliminate the need for unbounded gradients?

4. **Connection to VAE Optimization**: Theorem 4 is proven for the optimization problem (6), which follows from the VAE problem (3) only if σ_z is constant and γ is small. The requirement of constant σ_z and the transfer of conclusions to the VAE case need more rigorous justification.

**Incomplete Bayesian Perspective**: The optimization of γ refers to a Bayesian problem statement, yet the paper lacks a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand. A more thorough Bayesian treatment could provide better intuition about what is happening and how to avoid the infinite gradient effect with proper priors on parameters.

**Missing Comparative Analysis**: The paper uses insights from Dai & Wipf (2018) but provides insufficient discussion on how the new results relate to and extend that prior work. Additionally, the paper does not address whether the infinite gradients phenomenon occurs in other types of models. What makes this statement unique to VAEs? Do similar issues arise in Gaussian Mixture Models, Kernel PCA, Bayesian regression, or standard PCA? For example, in kernel PCA one could also require the data interpolation property—would we observe the same problem? These questions are relevant for understanding the generality of the findings.

## Minor Weaknesses

**Practical Guidance**: While the paper argues for accommodating unbounded gradients, it provides limited concrete guidance for practitioners. The suggestions to use "reduced step size" or "checks for oscillating gradient sign patterns" are vague. Specific algorithms for tuning γ beyond general advice are not provided. The paper also lacks discussion of computational costs and numerical stability considerations—how to prevent NaN/Inf values in practice remains underdeveloped.

**Generalization Beyond Training**: The theory addresses training set reconstruction but does not address test-set generalization. Do optimal sparse representations on training data generalize well? The relationship between optimal sparse representations and generalization performance remains unclear.

**Scalability and Modern Settings**: All experiments are on relatively small-scale problems (CelebA images at 128×128). Applicability to modern large-scale settings and current VAE variants is unclear. The paper does not discuss wall-clock time comparisons or computational overhead.

**β-VAE Analysis**: Section 2.3 introduces the β > d/r threshold but provides no empirical validation of this bound across datasets or architectures.

**Minor Notation Issues**:
- Line 103: Should it be "minimal" instead of "maximal"?
- Line 325: "spare representations" should likely be "sparse representations"
- Notation is sometimes heavy with multiple subscripts/superscripts

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide more extensive empirical validation across multiple datasets and architectures, including comparisons with bounded-gradient alternatives and gradient clipping strategies?

2. How does the theory extend to scenarios where the low-dimensional manifold assumption fails or is only approximately satisfied?

3. What specific guidance can you provide practitioners on when to accommodate versus constrain gradients in practice?

4. How do the theoretical results scale to modern large-scale VAE applications and non-Gaussian VAE variants?

5. Can you provide empirical validation of the β > d/r threshold and discuss when this condition is satisfied in practice?

## Significance and Impact

The work provides valuable theoretical perspective on a practical phenomenon in VAE training. Understanding that unbounded gradients serve a functional purpose rather than being mere artifacts is important for the field. However

reviews.summary: This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.
reviews.main_review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.

Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.

Main Review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the role of unbounded gradients in variational autoencoders (VAEs), particularly when the decoder variance parameter γ approaches zero during training. Rather than treating this phenomenon as a pathology to be avoided, the authors argue that infinite gradients are actually necessary for achieving optimal sparse latent representations on low-dimensional manifolds. The paper makes three main contributions: (1) a formal definition of optimal sparse representations as achieving zero reconstruction error while using the minimum number of informative latent dimensions; (2) a theoretical proof (Theorem 4) establishing that any autoencoder-based objective with bounded gradients cannot guarantee optimal sparse representations across all data and decoder configurations; and (3) empirical evidence that learning γ during training provides beneficial smoothing effects on the loss landscape, localizing problematic unbounded gradients to regions near convergence.

## Strengths

**Originality and Conceptual Contribution**: The paper makes a valuable and novel contribution by reframing unbounded gradients from a pathology to a necessity. This perspective shift is genuinely original and challenges conventional wisdom in VAE training. The theoretical result (Theorem 4) appears to be the first formal statement about the necessity of unbounded gradients for sparse representations, filling an important gap by addressing under-regularization—a regime that has received less attention than posterior collapse in prior literature.

**Theoretical Rigor**: The theoretical analysis is generally sound and carefully constructed. Theorem 4 is well-stated, and the proof strategy via counterexample is appropriate for establishing the necessity claim. The intuition provided in Section 3.3 and through Figures 2-3 effectively conveys why bounded gradients fail to guarantee optimal sparse representations. Lemmas 2-3 usefully connect the generic objective (Eq. 7) to VAE losses, establishing the relationship between theoretical constructs and practical VAE training. The paper is commendably transparent about the limitations of its theoretical framework, acknowledging that Definition 1 involves restrictive assumptions and noting exceptions (e.g., adversarial outliers in Section 2.2).

**Clarity and Presentation**: The paper is well-written with clear motivation and logical flow. The toy illustrations (Figures 1-2) effectively convey intuition about why sparse representations require unbounded gradients. The progression from practical motivation to theoretical results is natural and accessible. Definition 1 precisely articulates the notion of optimal sparse representation, and the discussion of β-VAE in Section 2.3 provides useful context.

## Major Weaknesses

**Limited Scope of Theoretical Results**: While Theorem 4 is the paper's centerpiece, its applicability is narrower than the claims might suggest. The theorem applies specifically to a class of generic AE objectives (Eq. 7) and relies on a somewhat artificial counterexample construction—a labyrinthine manifold design—rather than a constructive proof providing intuitive insight. The theorem establishes necessity but not sufficiency; the paper does not characterize when bounded-gradient objectives might still work in practice or under what conditions the theoretical guarantees actually hold. Additionally, the connection between the generic objective and actual VAE training relies on Lemmas 2-3, which require limiting conditions (γ→0, σz→0) and restrictive assumptions (linear decoders, constant posterior variance) that may not hold during finite training with nonlinear decoders.

**Restrictive Assumptions**: Definition 1's assumption of perfect reconstruction (zero error) is unrealistic for real data with noise or inherent measurement uncertainty. While the paper acknowledges this in Remark 2, the gap between theory and practice remains substantial. More critically, the entire analysis assumes: (i) data lies on a known low-dimensional manifold, which real data often violates; (ii) decoders possess sufficient capacity to perfectly reconstruct manifold data, an assumption that is strong and empirically unverified; (iii) deterministic encoders (Definition 1), though VAEs use stochastic encoders—Remark 4 addresses this but the connection feels loose. These assumptions collectively limit the practical applicability of the theoretical results.

**Insufficient Experimental Validation**: The experimental section is notably limited. The main empirical contribution is Figure 4, comparing learned γ versus fixed γ on CelebA, but this evaluation is narrow in scope: only one dataset is examined, only two configurations are compared, metrics are limited (reconstruction error and MMD), and there are no comparisons to alternative methods for handling unbounded gradients (e.g., gradient clipping, second-order optimization methods, fixed small γ with adaptive learning rates, or alternative VAE variants like β-VAE with tuned β). The paper lacks ablation studies examining sensitivity to network architecture, latent dimension κ, or initialization choices. The outlier detection application (Figure 1, supplementary material) is illustrative but not rigorously evaluated. All experiments are on relatively small-scale problems (CelebA at 128×128), leaving applicability to modern large-scale settings unclear. Additionally, the β>d/r threshold mentioned in Section 2.3 is never empirically validated across datasets.

**Limited Practical Guidance**: Although the paper argues that unbounded gradients are necessary, it provides limited concrete guidance on preventing numerical instabilities in practice. The suggestions to use "reduced step size" or "checks for oscillating gradient sign patterns" are vague and underdeveloped. The paper does not discuss computational costs, wall-clock time comparisons, or specific implementation details. Practitioners are told to accommodate large gradients but offered minimal concrete techniques beyond acknowledging existing approaches. The paper would benefit from explicit recommendations on when to accommodate versus constrain gradients and how to do so reliably.

## Minor Weaknesses

**Technical Concerns**: Several technical issues warrant attention. First, the paper does not address how finite precision arithmetic affects the theoretical guarantees about infinite gradients—in practice, numerical overflow and underflow are real concerns. Second, the relationship between global minima satisfying Definition 1 and actual convergence behavior of stochastic gradient descent remains unclear; optimal reconstruction on training data doesn't guarantee good test-set generalization. Third, the assumption that the intrinsic manifold dimension r is known or easily estimated is not addressed. Fourth, notation is sometimes heavy with multiple subscripts and superscripts, potentially limiting accessibility.

**Limited Scope of Applicability**: The analysis explicitly addresses manifold data (low intrinsic dimension) but applicability to other data regimes—high-dimensional data, data with complex structure not captured by manifold models, discrete or mixed-type data—remains unclear. The paper assumes Gaussian VAE architectures with specific decoder parameterizations; extensions to non-Gaussian posteriors or modern VAE variants (hierarchical VAEs, normalizing flows) are mentioned only briefly in the conclusion. The outlier detection application assumes outliers don't lie on the inlier manifold, a strong assumption often violated in practice.

**Missing Comparisons and Context**: The paper does not empirically demonstrate that bounded-gradient alternatives fail in practice, only that they fail in principle for certain theoretical constructions. A more direct comparison showing that bounded-gradient objectives consistently underperform across diverse settings would strengthen the claims. Additionally, recent work on VAE posterior collapse and sparsity could be engaged more thoroughly. The relationship between optimal sparse representations and downstream task performance (e.g., classification, anomaly detection) is not explored.

**Reproducibility Concerns**: While the paper provides sufficient mathematical detail for theoretical results, experimental reproducibility is limited. CelebA experiments lack detailed hyperparameters (network architecture, learning rates, batch sizes, number of runs, random seeds). No code is mentioned as available. The toy example in Figure 3 is not fully specified. Supplementary material (referenced multiple times) is not provided for review, making complete assessment difficult.

## Questions and Suggestions for Authors

1. **Initialization Effects**: The paper studies learning decoder variance initialized at γ=1. Have you considered other initializations—for instance, initializing γ at its eventual convergence value while still keeping it learnable? The dynamics in Figure 4b suggest this could be an interesting ablation. How sensitive are the results to initialization?

2. **Sample Quality and Two-Stage Training**: You claim that learnable γ is important for improving sample quality, yet learnable γ results in very small values, potentially leaving the VAE under-regularized. How do you reconcile this with the fact that direct sampling from such a model should not lead to good sample quality? If you employ two-stage training (training a generative model on the aggregated posterior), are the resulting samples truly samples from the VAE, or are they samples from a different model? Please clarify this distinction.

3. **OOD Detection with Fixed Small γ**: In your OOD detection experiments, since VAE log-likelihood is largely dominated by reconstruction loss, and you show that learning γ and fixing γ at a small value yield similar reconstruction loss, do you observe similar OOD detection performance when γ is fixed at a small value? Intuitively, they should behave similarly. If they differ, what accounts for the difference?

4. **Practical Necessity**: Can you provide empirical evidence that bounded-gradient alternatives (e.g., with other regularizers or

reviews.summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly. 

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

The paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model, where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero (particularly for high-capacity models and noise-free data), which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper argues that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (which implies a high-fidelity generative model) near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e. using unnecessary latent dimensions). Finally the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA

**Contributions:**

1) Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. Significance: unsurprisingly, the source of the problem is the Gaussian likelihood term, which is unbounded for the variance approaching zero. The latter has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature and is a well known pathology when fitting Gaussian (mixture) models. 

2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.
reviews.main_review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the role of unbounded gradients in variational autoencoders (VAEs) with Gaussian likelihood and learnable variance, arguing that such gradients—which arise when the variance parameter γ approaches zero—should be embraced rather than mitigated. The authors contend that under specific assumptions (Gaussian likelihood, fixed latent-space prior, noise-free data on low-dimensional manifolds), near-unbounded gradients are necessary for learning optimal sparse representations defined as achieving zero reconstruction error while using the minimal number of informative latent dimensions. The paper provides three main contributions: (1) a formal definition of optimal sparse representations, (2) a theoretical proof (Theorem 4) that any autoencoder-based objective with bounded gradients cannot guarantee optimal sparse representations across all data and decoder configurations, and (3) evidence that learning γ during training provides a smoothing effect that localizes problematic gradients to regions near convergence while preserving their benefits.

## Originality, Quality, and Significance

The paper addresses an underexplored aspect of VAE training and presents a genuinely novel perspective by reframing unbounded gradients from a pathology to a necessity. This conceptual contribution is valuable and challenges established wisdom in the field. The theoretical analysis is generally rigorous, with Theorem 4 as the centerpiece establishing a formal connection between bounded gradients and the impossibility of optimal sparse representations. However, several important caveats apply to the claimed significance.

**Scope of Claims:** The paper's central claims are stated in broad terms that suggest applicability to general autoencoder architectures, but the theoretical results strictly apply only to Gaussian-likelihood VAEs with trainable variance and fixed latent-space priors. This critical limitation is not sufficiently emphasized throughout the manuscript. The pathology of unbounded gradients fundamentally stems from the unbounded nature of the Gaussian likelihood as variance approaches zero—a well-known issue in the broader statistical literature on Gaussian mixture models. Importantly, this problem does not apply to alternative likelihood models (e.g., Bernoulli likelihoods, flow-based models) that do not exhibit this unboundedness property. The paper should explicitly clarify that the findings are specific to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with similar unbounded behavior, rather than suggesting they apply to "any AE architecture" as currently implied.

**Definition of Optimality:** The definition of optimal sparse representations (Definition 1) as achieving zero reconstruction error is problematic and overstated in its generality. While lossless compression may be appropriate for specific applications (such as training high-fidelity generative models on noise-free data), there are numerous well-established contexts where lossy compression is clearly desirable and necessary. These include representation learning, disentanglement learning, outlier detection with noisy data, and any task operating on real-world data containing noise and task-irrelevant variations. The information bottleneck literature (Tishby et al., 2000), rate-distortion theory, and recent work on fixing broken ELBOs (Alemi et al., 2017, 2018) all demonstrate that optimal representations often involve non-zero reconstruction error. The paper acknowledges this restrictiveness in remarks but does not provide adequate discussion of when lossless versus lossy compression is appropriate. Section 2.2's treatment of outlier detection, for instance, assumes data lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model—a rarely-satisfied assumption in practice that deserves more nuanced discussion.

**Theoretical Contributions:** Theorem 4 is technically sound but relies on a specific counterexample construction rather than a general necessity proof. While the proof strategy is valid, the counterexample involves a somewhat artificial labyrinthine manifold construction, and the generalization to typical deep learning scenarios remains unclear. Moreover, the theorem establishes necessity but not sufficiency of unbounded gradients—the paper does not characterize conditions under which bounded-gradient objectives might still perform adequately in practice, nor does it provide empirical evidence that bounded alternatives consistently fail outside the theoretical construction.

The connection between the generic objective (Eq. 7) and actual VAE training (Lemmas 2-3) relies on limiting conditions (γ→0, σz→0) and restrictive functional forms (linear decoders, specific penalty structures) that may not hold exactly during finite training. The gap between these theoretical idealized conditions and practical finite-capacity networks operating with finite precision is substantial and deserves more thorough discussion.

## Critical Limitations and Conceptual Issues

**Applicability and Assumptions:** The analysis assumes several strong conditions: (1) decoders have sufficient capacity to perfectly reconstruct data on a known low-dimensional manifold, (2) data is noise-free or near-noise-free, (3) the intrinsic dimensionality of the data is known or determinable, and (4) the Gaussian likelihood with fixed prior is the appropriate modeling choice. Real-world data often violates these assumptions. The paper should discuss how robustness degrades when these conditions are violated and provide guidance on recognizing when the theoretical framework applies.

The assumption that optimal representations should achieve zero reconstruction error is particularly problematic. For many practical tasks, perfect reconstruction is undesirable and even harmful. The paper should include a dedicated discussion (at least a paragraph) contrasting when zero-error reconstruction is appropriate versus when lossy compression is preferable, with references to the substantial literature on this topic. This discussion should acknowledge that the definition of optimality is task-dependent.

**Alternative Approaches Not Adequately Addressed:** The paper claims (Line 327) that "there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal," but this statement overlooks important alternatives. Sparsity can be achieved through aggregate posteriors (as in Tomczak & Welling, 2018, and the Deep Variational Information Bottleneck) where the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal posterior. This approach enables lossy compression with automatic sparsity without requiring unbounded gradients. The paper should discuss why the authors believe learning γ is preferable to these alternatives, or acknowledge their validity for alternative objectives.

Furthermore, the paper does not adequately engage with practical alternatives for handling unbounded gradients: gradient clipping strategies, second-order optimization methods, fixed small γ with adaptive learning rates, and other VAE variants (β-VAE with tuned β, free-bits, annealing strategies). Without empirical comparisons demonstrating that these alternatives fail in practice, the claim that unbounded gradients are necessary remains somewhat theoretical.

**Modeling Choices:** The paper questions whether VAEs are appropriate for high-fidelity generative modeling with zero reconstruction error, yet this is precisely the setting where unbounded gradients become most problematic. The authors should discuss why alternative modeling choices—flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, aggregate posteriors, or other likelihood models that don't suffer from unbounded gradients—might be more appropriate for this goal. This discussion would help readers understand when to apply the paper's framework versus when to consider alternative approaches.

## Experimental Validation

The experimental evidence supporting the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is limited and insufficient to validate the broader claims. The main empirical contribution (Figure 4) compares learned γ versus fixed γ exclusively on CelebA at 128×128 resolution. This single dataset is insufficient to establish generalizability. The evaluation lacks:

- Multiple datasets spanning different domains and characteristics
- Diverse network architectures and capacity levels
- Comparison with alternative regularization schemes and bounded-gradient objectives
- Multiple independent runs with statistical significance testing (though the margins do appear reasonable)
- Ablation studies examining sensitivity to initialization, latent dimension κ, and other hyperparameters
- Evaluation on high-resolution or high-intrinsic-dimension data
- Computational cost analysis and wall-clock time comparisons

The outlier detection application (Section 2.2, Figure 1) is mentioned but not rigorously evaluated. The example assumes outliers don't lie on the inlier manifold—a strong assumption that often fails in practice with adversarial or naturally-occurring outliers. The paper's own acknowledgment that this assumption "rarely holds in practice" (Remark 1) substantially limits the practical applicability of this application.

The β-VAE analysis introduces a threshold β > d/r but provides no empirical validation of this bound across datasets. The toy example in Figure 3 is helpful for intuition but is not fully specified, limiting reproducibility.

## Technical and Clarity Issues

**Scope Clarification:** Throughout the paper, claims should be explicitly qualified with the specific assumptions under which they hold. For instance, Line 219-220's statement about when sparse representations require unbounded gradients should be weakened or clarified to apply specifically to Gaussian likelihoods, fixed priors, and Definition 1's notion of optimality. The paper's framing often suggests broader applicability than the technical results support.

**Generalization Beyond Training:** The paper addresses training set reconstruction but does not analyze test-set generalization. Do optimal sparse representations (zero reconstruction error on training data) generalize well to held-out data? This gap is particularly important given that the paper's definition of optimality focuses on training
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly. 

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

The paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model, where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero (particularly for high-capacity models and noise-free data), which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper argues that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (which implies a high-fidelity generative model) near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e. using unnecessary latent dimensions). Finally the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA

**Contributions:**

1) Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. Significance: unsurprisingly, the source of the problem is the Gaussian likelihood term, which is unbounded for the variance approaching zero. The latter has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature and is a well known pathology when fitting Gaussian (mixture) models. 

2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.

Main Review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

===== After Rebuttal =====

Please refer to my "After Rebuttal" post.

=====================

~~This is clearly not a NeurIPS level submission. I know it is quite frustrating to receive a rejection, so I have patiently read this manuscript until page 5, trying my best to prepare as much feedback as possible to help the author(s) improve this work, then I gave up. Its contents are simply not technically sound. I scrolled down to the Experiment section and found out it is not adequate as well, so here I am writing this review. The language issues in writing (sentences too long, grammar issues, bad expressions, etc.) I can tolerate them, but there are factual errors in this paper, so my evaluation is a firm no. The author(s) have some fundamental misunderstanding of variational inference models, as reflected in the definitions, analysis, and examples presented in this work. This work will be of limited value to the community. See more detailed comments in the limitation section below.~~
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
- Infinite gradients or overfitting? It turned out the infinite gradient issue the author(s) heavily focused on is caused by letting the precision parameter of the Gaussian noise model be trainable, which always explodes to infinity. Well, it is well known this is simply the overfitting in the finite sample scenario, and of course, it will drive the gradient to infinity. 
- The PCA example is inconsistent with the optimal sparse representation definition, as its latent representation has no uncertainty at all. 
- Reconstruction error is just an intuitive explanation of likelihood loss under the Gaussian observation model. For a proper theory of variational inference, the concepts should be instead defined by conditional likelihoods. 
- Please use vector formats instead of bitmaps in the Figures.
- The experiments need to be substantially enriched. 
- I do not feel the author(s) have shed real valuable insights to guide practice. What does the statement "this result suggests that heuristic modifications to or constraints on the VAE energy function may be ill-advised, and large gradients should be accommodated to the extent possible." even mean? Such confusing.
